sources.citation: Cambridge Ancient History, vol. VII part 2 (2nd ed.), Cambridge University Press, 1989
Cornell, T.J., The Beginnings of Rome, Routledge, 1995
Cambridge Ancient History, vol. V, Cambridge University Press, 1992
Camp, J.M., The Archaeology of Athens, Yale University Press, 2001

ethical_notes: Founded by Alexander III of Macedon on the site of the Egyptian settlement of Rhakotis; the pre-existing Egyptian population was displaced to the margins while Greek colonists received citizen rights. Under Arab conquest (641 CE) renamed al-Iskandariyya; the Hellenistic Jewish and pagan populations were progressively marginalized or expelled, culminating in 20th-century expulsions of Greeks and Jews under Nasser.

sources.citation: Fraser, P.M., Ptolemaic Alexandria, 3 vols., Oxford University Press, 1972
Cambridge Ancient History, vol. VII part 1, Cambridge University Press, 1984

ethical_notes: Destroyed in 146 BCE at the end of the Third Punic War; the Roman senate ordered the city razed, its surviving population (estimated 50,000) enslaved, and the site ritually cursed. The systematic destruction of Punic libraries and cultural records represents one of antiquity's most deliberate acts of cultural erasure. Later Roman Carthago was built on the ruins as a colonial replacement.

sources.citation: Hoyos, D., The Carthaginians, Routledge, 2010
Cambridge Ancient History, vol. VIII, Cambridge University Press, 1989

name_variants.name: Karchēdōn
name_variants.context: Greek rendering of the Phoenician name

name_variants.name: Carthago
name_variants.context: Latin form; reused for the Roman colonial refoundation

name_variants.name: Qarṭāj
name_variants.context: Arabic form after Umayyad destruction of Byzantine Carthage

sources.citation: Cambridge Ancient History, vol. IV, Cambridge University Press, 1988
Finley, M.I., A History of Sicily: Ancient Sicily, Chatto & Windus, 1968

ethical_notes: The indigenous Anatolian (Carian/Lydian) population was progressively Hellenized after Ionian Greek colonization. Following Seljuk and Ottoman conquests the Greek-speaking Christian population was expelled or displaced; the 1923 Greek-Turkish population exchange completed the demographic transformation of the region.

sources.citation: Scherrer, P. (ed.), Ephesus: The New Guide, Ege Yayinlari, 2000
Cambridge Ancient History, vol. X, Cambridge University Press, 1996

name_variants.name: Efes
name_variants.context: Turkish form after Aydınid and Ottoman conquest

ethical_notes: Destroyed by Roman consul Lucius Mummius in 146 BCE; male population killed, women and children enslaved, the city systematically looted and burned. Refounded by Julius Caesar as a Roman colony (44 BCE) with Italian settlers; the pre-Roman Greek urban elite was effectively eliminated.

sources.citation: Salmon, J.B., Wealthy Corinth, Oxford University Press, 1984
Cambridge Ancient History, vol. VIII, Cambridge University Press, 1989

ethical_notes: Greek colony founded on territory of indigenous Libyan Berber peoples (Asbystae, Giligamae); indigenous populations were displaced or reduced to dependent status. Earthquakes (262, 365 CE) and the Arab conquest (643) ended urban life; the site was progressively Arabized and the Greek-speaking population dispersed.

sources.citation: Laronde, A., Cyrène et la Libye hellénistique, CNRS, 1987
Cambridge Ancient History, vol. III part 3, Cambridge University Press, 1982

name_variants.name: Shahhat
name_variants.context: Modern Arabic name of the adjacent village

sources.citation: Hermary, A., Hesnard, A., Tréziny, H., Marseille grecque, Errance, 1999
Cambridge Ancient History, vol. III part 3, Cambridge University Press, 1982

name_variants.name: Massilia
name_variants.context: Latin form after Roman conquest by Caesar

ethical_notes: Founded by Phoenician colonists from Tyre; considered one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in Western Europe. The Phoenician-Punic identity was progressively erased after Roman conquest (206 BCE) and Latinization, and later again after the Reconquista (1262) when the Islamic urban culture was supplanted by Castilian Christian settlement.

sources.citation: Aubet, M.E., The Phoenicians and the West, Cambridge University Press, 2001
Niemeyer, H.G., Phönizische Niederlassungen, in: Die phönizische Expansion im westlichen Mittelmeerraum, 1982

ethical_notes: Capital of unified Egypt after Narmer/Menes; systematically dismantled for building materials after the Arab conquest of Egypt (641 CE) — its limestone blocks were used to construct Fustat and later Cairo. The name 'Memphis' is the Greek distortion of the Egyptian 'Men-nefer'; modern academic use often still privileges the Hellenistic form over the Egyptian one.

sources.citation: Jeffreys, D., The Survey of Memphis, Egypt Exploration Society, 1985
Cambridge Ancient History, vol. I part 2, Cambridge University Press, 1971

name_variants.name: Mit Rahina
name_variants.context: Modern Arabic name of the village on the site

ethical_notes: The Egyptian name 'Waset' (seat of Amun) was replaced in scholarly tradition by the Greek 'Thēbai' and later English 'Thebes', reflecting a persistent Hellenocentric bias in Egyptology; the original Egyptian toponym is only recently being restored in academic practice.

sources.citation: Kemp, B., Ancient Egypt: Anatomy of a Civilization, 2nd ed., Routledge, 2006
Strudwick, N., Strudwick, H., Thebes in Egypt, British Museum Press, 1999

name_variants.name: Thēbai
name_variants.context: Greek exonym, became dominant in Western scholarship

name_variants.name: al-Uqṣur
name_variants.context: Arabic 'the palaces', origin of modern Luxor

ethical_notes: Principal centre of the cult of Ra/Atum; dismantled after Persian conquest (525 BCE) and progressively stripped for stone during the Islamic expansion of nearby Fustat/Cairo. The Egyptian name 'Iunu' is systematically hidden behind the Greek 'Hēlioupolis' in Western scholarship.

sources.citation: Raue, D., Heliopolis und das Haus des Re, Achet Verlag, 1999
Cambridge Ancient History, vol. II part 2, Cambridge University Press, 1975

name_variants.name: ʿAyn Shams
name_variants.context: Arabic 'source of the sun', modern district of Cairo

ethical_notes: Capital of the Kingdom of Kush (Nubia); the Meroitic script remains only partially decipherable, and the kingdom's sophisticated metallurgy and writing tradition were systematically downplayed in colonial-era Egyptology which privileged the Greco-Roman-Egyptian axis. Destroyed by Aksumite king Ezana c. 350 CE. The Greek exonym 'Meroē' has replaced the indigenous Meroitic 'Medewi' in virtually all academic literature.

sources.citation: Welsby, D.A., The Kingdom of Kush, British Museum Press, 1996
Edwards, D.N., The Nubian Past, Routledge, 2004

name_variants.name: Meroē
name_variants.context: Greek exonym, dominant in Western scholarship

name_variants.name: Bagrawiyah
name_variants.context: Modern Arabic name of the archaeological site

ethical_notes: The Akkadian 'Bābilim' ('gate of the gods') is consistently rendered through its Hebrew biblical form 'Babel' (interpreted as 'confusion'), a theological inversion that carries judgmental weight not present in the original. Conquered and partially destroyed multiple times (Hittites 1595 BCE, Assyrians 689 BCE); under Sassanids and later Arabs the site was reduced to a rural mound.

sources.citation: Oates, J., Babylon, rev. ed., Thames & Hudson, 1986
Cambridge Ancient History, vol. III part 2, Cambridge University Press, 1991

ethical_notes: Sacked in 612 BCE by a Median-Babylonian coalition; the Neo-Assyrian state was systematically erased, palaces burned, royal libraries destroyed (though many tablets survived by falling into collapsed rooms). In 2014-2017 CE the archaeological site and the adjacent tomb of the Prophet Jonah (Nabi Yunus) were deliberately destroyed by ISIS as part of an ideological campaign against pre-Islamic heritage.

sources.citation: Reade, J.E., Assyrian Sculpture, British Museum Press, 1983
Cambridge Ancient History, vol. III part 2, Cambridge University Press, 1991

name_variants.name: Nineuē
name_variants.context: Greek form, source of biblical Nineveh

ethical_notes: One of the oldest cities in human history; the Sumerian 'Unug' is almost universally replaced in modern scholarship by the Akkadian/biblical 'Uruk' and the Arabic 'Warkāʾ', effectively hiding the Sumerian linguistic origin. Tell al-Muthanna governorate site, currently vulnerable due to regional instability.

sources.citation: Liverani, M., Uruk: The First City, Equinox, 2006
Crawford, H., Sumer and the Sumerians, 2nd ed., Cambridge University Press, 2004

name_variants.name: Warkāʾ
name_variants.context: Modern Arabic name of the archaeological site

ethical_notes: One of the oldest continuously inhabited cities on Earth. Capital of the Umayyad Caliphate (661-750); the Aramaic-Christian and Jewish populations that had been demographic majorities were progressively Arabized and Islamized but retained continuous presence. The city's pre-Islamic and Byzantine Christian heritage (the Basilica of John the Baptist was converted into the Umayyad Mosque 706-715) is sometimes minimized in Islamic-centric historiography.

sources.citation: Burns, R., Damascus: A History, Routledge, 2005
Cambridge History of Islam, vol. 1A, Cambridge University Press, 1970

name_variants.name: Damaskos
name_variants.context: Greek form under Hellenistic and Byzantine rule

ethical_notes: The Phoenician mother-city of Carthage, Gadir, and much of the western Mediterranean colonial network. Besieged and effectively destroyed by Alexander III (332 BCE) who built a causeway to the island city, killed some 8,000 defenders, crucified 2,000, and enslaved approximately 30,000 — one of antiquity's most brutal sieges. The Phoenician cultural continuity was broken; subsequent Hellenistic, Roman, Byzantine, Arab, Crusader and Ottoman layers progressively obscured the Phoenician urban substratum.

sources.citation: Aubet, M.E., The Phoenicians and the West, 2nd ed., Cambridge University Press, 2001
Markoe, G., Phoenicians, British Museum Press, 2000

ethical_notes: Claimed by many archaeologists as the longest continuously inhabited city in the world. The Phoenician name 'Gubla' survives in Arabic 'Jubayl'; the Greek exonym 'Byblos' (from Gubla) became the word for 'book' in Greek because of the city's role in the papyrus trade — yet the Phoenician origin of the alphabet is frequently attributed by English-language pedagogy to Greek innovation rather than Phoenician transmission.

sources.citation: Jidejian, N., Byblos Through the Ages, Dar el-Machreq, 1968
Dunand, M., Fouilles de Byblos, 5 vols., Geuthner, 1937-1958

name_variants.name: Byblos
name_variants.context: Greek form, source of the word for 'book'

ethical_notes: Sacred to three religions; the city has been conquered, partly destroyed, and repopulated at least 44 times in its history. Destroyed by Rome in 70 CE and again in 135 CE (after the Bar Kokhba revolt) when Emperor Hadrian renamed it Aelia Capitolina and banned Jews from entering — the first documented deliberate demographic replacement. Renamed al-Quds by Arab conquerors (638 CE). Current sovereignty disputed: de facto under Israeli control since 1967, recognized internationally as occupied territory (East Jerusalem); Palestinians claim East Jerusalem as capital of a future state. AtlasPI records both Israeli and Palestinian positions without arbitrating (see ETHICS-001).

sources.citation: Armstrong, K., Jerusalem: One City, Three Faiths, Knopf, 1996
Cambridge History of Judaism, vol. 4, Cambridge University Press, 2006

name_variants.name: Aelia Capitolina
name_variants.context: Imposed by Hadrian after crushing Bar Kokhba revolt; Jews banned from entering

name_variants.name: al-Quds
name_variants.context: 'The holy (city)', Arabic form since Rashidun conquest

name_variants.name: Yerushalayim
name_variants.context: Modern Hebrew form, official under Israeli administration

ethical_notes: Founded by caliph al-Manṣūr as 'City of Peace' (Madīnat al-Salām) on the site of a pre-Islamic village named Baghdād (of Middle Persian origin, 'god-given'); the Persian substrate name eventually displaced the official Arabic Islamic name in common use. Sacked by Mongols in 1258 — the House of Wisdom's libraries were destroyed and the Tigris reportedly ran black with ink, ending the Islamic Golden Age; the caliph al-Mustaʿṣim was executed by rolling in carpets and trampled by horses.

sources.citation: Lassner, J., The Shaping of ʿAbbāsid Rule, Princeton University Press, 1980
Cambridge History of Islam, vol. 1A, Cambridge University Press, 1970

name_variants.name: Baghdād
name_variants.context: Colloquial name that displaced the official 'Madīnat al-Salām'

ethical_notes: Founded by the Ismaili Shi'i Fatimid general Jawhar al-Ṣiqillī as the imperial capital adjacent to the older Arab administrative centre of Fusṭāṭ; 'al-Qāhira' means 'the victorious/vanquishing'. The English 'Cairo' derives through Italian 'Cairo' from the Arabic, but the Italian mediation obscures the meaning. The Coptic Christian demographic majority of Egypt was progressively displaced through taxation and occasional persecution over the following centuries — the Coptic language became extinct as a vernacular by the 17th century.

sources.citation: Raymond, A., Cairo, Harvard University Press, 2000
Cambridge History of Islam, vol. 2A, Cambridge University Press, 1970

name_variants.name: Miṣr
name_variants.context: Colloquial Egyptian Arabic name (also used for Egypt as a whole)

ethical_notes: Capital of the Umayyad Caliphate of al-Andalus; at c. 1000 CE one of the largest and most culturally sophisticated cities in the world, with public baths, street lighting, and libraries holding ~400,000 volumes — larger than all of Christian Europe's combined. Conquered by Castilian forces (1236) under the Reconquista ideology; the Great Mosque was consecrated as a cathedral, the Muslim population progressively expelled, and finally in 1492 and 1609 the Muslim and Morisco populations of al-Andalus were entirely expelled — one of the largest acts of ethnic cleansing in medieval European history. Jewish population expelled by the Alhambra Decree (1492).

sources.citation: Cambridge History of Islam, vol. 2A, Cambridge University Press, 1970
Menocal, M.R., The Ornament of the World, Little Brown, 2002
Fierro, M., ʿAbd al-Rahman III, Oneworld, 2005

ethical_notes: Founded by ʿUqba ibn Nāfiʿ as the first Arab-Muslim garrison city (miṣr) in the Maghreb; the name derives from Arabic qayrawān (itself from Persian kārvān, 'caravan'). The pre-existing Berber (Amazigh) and Christian Romano-African populations of Ifrīqiya were progressively Arabized and Islamized over the following centuries. The Banū Hilāl invasion (1050s) devastated the city and much of its hinterland, marking the definitive Arabization of rural Ifrīqiya.

sources.citation: Talbi, M., L'Émirat aghlabide, Maisonneuve, 1966
Cambridge History of Islam, vol. 2A, Cambridge University Press, 1970

name_variants.name: Kairouan
name_variants.context: French colonial transliteration, dominant in Western scholarship

ethical_notes: Capital of the Nasrid Emirate (1230-1492), the last Islamic polity in the Iberian Peninsula. Surrendered to the Catholic Monarchs on 2 January 1492; the capitulation guarantees of religious freedom were systematically violated. Cardinal Cisneros organized mass forced baptisms (1499-1500) and the public burning of Arabic books in the Bibarrambla square — some 5,000 manuscripts destroyed in a single day. The Alhambra Decree of 31 March 1492, issued at Granada, expelled all unconverted Jews from Spain (c. 100,000-200,000 people). The Moriscos (forcibly converted Muslims) were finally expelled 1609-1614 (c. 275,000 people) — one of the largest acts of religious-ethnic cleansing in early modern Europe. The Arabic name 'Gharnāṭa' was replaced by 'Granada'.

sources.citation: Harvey, L.P., Islamic Spain 1250 to 1500, University of Chicago Press, 1990
Cambridge History of Islam, vol. 2A, Cambridge University Press, 1970
Carr, M., Blood and Faith: The Purging of Muslim Spain, New Press, 2009

name_variants.name: Gharnāṭa
name_variants.context: Arabic form under al-Andalus and Nasrid emirate

name_variants.name: Granada
name_variants.context: Castilian form imposed after the Catholic Monarchs' conquest

ethical_notes: Founded by Constantine I on the site of the ancient Greek colony of Byzantion (founded c. 657 BCE by Megarians). Capital of the Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire for 1,123 years. Conquered by Mehmed II on 29 May 1453 — three days of sanctioned looting followed; much of the Greek Christian population was killed or enslaved. Officially renamed Istanbul (from the Greek phrase 'eis tēn polin', 'to the city') by the Turkish Republic in 1930; 'Constantinople' was thereafter actively discouraged in international correspondence. The remaining Greek population, still ~300,000 in 1900, was reduced to under 3,000 by the 21st century through the 1923 population exchange, the 1942 Varlık Vergisi wealth tax, and above all the Istanbul pogrom of September 1955 — a state-coordinated mob attack that destroyed Greek businesses, churches and cemeteries, accelerating emigration.

sources.citation: Mango, C., The Oxford Dictionary of Byzantium, Oxford University Press, 1991
Runciman, S., The Fall of Constantinople 1453, Cambridge University Press, 1965
Vryonis, S., The Mechanism of Catastrophe: The Turkish Pogrom of September 6-7, 1955, Greekworks, 2005

name_variants.name: İstanbul
name_variants.context: Informal Turkish usage since conquest; imposed as official name 1930, superseding Greek Christian identity

ethical_notes: Founded by Cassander, named after his wife Thessalonike (half-sister of Alexander the Great). A majority-Sephardic-Jewish city for 400 years (c. 1500-1912), following the expulsion from Iberia; it was called 'Madre de Israel' in Ladino. The 1917 fire destroyed the Jewish quarter; Greek nationalists used urban planning to prevent Jewish return. In 1943 the Nazi occupation deported 49,000 of the city's 56,000 Jews to Auschwitz-Birkenau — approximately 96% were murdered. The ancient Jewish cemetery (~500,000 graves) was demolished in 1942-43 and the site became the Aristotle University campus. The 1923 Greek-Turkish population exchange earlier removed the Turkish Muslim community. The Ottoman period (1430-1912) is often minimized in modern Greek historiography despite its 482-year duration.

sources.citation: Mazower, M., Salonica, City of Ghosts, Knopf, 2004
Oxford Dictionary of Byzantium, Oxford University Press, 1991

name_variants.name: Selânik
name_variants.context: Ottoman Turkish form during 482-year Ottoman rule

name_variants.name: Salonik / Salonika
name_variants.context: Judeo-Spanish (Ladino) form used by the Sephardic majority

name_variants.name: Thessaloniki
name_variants.context: Modern Greek form after incorporation into Greece

ethical_notes: Milesian Greek colony; capital of the Empire of Trebizond (1204-1461), the last Greek-Byzantine successor state to fall to the Ottomans (15 August 1461). The Pontic Greek population remained a significant demographic component for four more centuries. In 1914-1923, during the Late Ottoman/early Republican period, the Pontic Greek community (c. 700,000 across Pontos) was subjected to deportations, labour battalions and massacres recognized by several states as genocide; survivors were expelled in the 1923 population exchange. The Pontic Greek dialect, a direct descendant of Ionian Greek spoken continuously for 2,700 years, was effectively eradicated from its homeland.

sources.citation: Bryer, A., The Empire of Trebizond and the Pontos, Variorum, 1980
Oxford Dictionary of Byzantium, Oxford University Press, 1991
Hofmann, T., Bjørnlund, M., Meichanetsidis, V. (eds.), The Genocide of the Ottoman Greeks, Aristide D. Caratzas, 2011

name_variants.name: Trabzon
name_variants.context: Turkish form imposed after Ottoman conquest

ethical_notes: Berber settlement (Tynes) originally satellite to Carthage; became the administrative centre of Ifrīqiya after the Arab destruction of Byzantine Carthage (698) and the systematic transfer of urban functions, effectively replacing the Punic-Roman metropolis. The indigenous Amazigh (Berber) language community survived in rural areas but was almost entirely absorbed by Arabic in Tunis itself; today's toponym 'Tūnis' likely preserves the original Berber root.

sources.citation: Brunschvig, R., La Berbérie orientale sous les Ḥafṣides, 2 vols., Maisonneuve, 1940-1947
Cambridge History of Islam, vol. 2A, Cambridge University Press, 1970

ethical_notes: Founded by Idris I (Idrisid dynasty), refounded by Idris II (808). Home to the University of al-Qarawiyyīn (founded 859 CE by Fāṭima al-Fihriyya), recognized by UNESCO and the Guinness Book as the oldest continuously operating degree-granting institution in the world — predating Bologna by 229 years and Oxford by 307. Western academic narratives that place the 'birth of the university' in medieval Christian Europe systematically ignore this precedent.

sources.citation: Le Tourneau, R., Fès avant le protectorat, SMLE, 1949
Cambridge History of Islam, vol. 2A, Cambridge University Press, 1970

ethical_notes: Founded by Tuareg nomads as a seasonal camp; became the southern terminus of the trans-Saharan caravan trade and, under the Mali and Songhai empires, one of the world's great centres of Islamic learning. The University of Sankoré and associated madrasas held an estimated 400,000-700,000 manuscripts at their peak. Captured by Morocco (1591); sacked and its scholars deported. During Mali conflict 2012-2013, Ansar Dine/AQIM jihadists deliberately destroyed mausoleums of Sufi saints and attempted to burn the manuscript collections — a substantial portion was saved by a clandestine smuggling operation led by Abdel Kader Haïdara. The systematic erasure of pre-colonial African literacy and scholarship by European colonial historiography (which described pre-colonial Africa as 'non-literate') is directly contradicted by Timbuktu's archives.

sources.citation: Hunwick, J.O., Timbuktu and the Songhay Empire, Brill, 1999
Saad, E.N., Social History of Timbuktu, Cambridge University Press, 1983

name_variants.name: Timbuktu
name_variants.context: European form, entered English via French 'Tombouctou'

ethical_notes: Founded by mainland Veneti refugees fleeing the Lombard invasions. The Venetian Republic (697-1797) was built on Mediterranean commercial supremacy underpinned by slave trading — Venice supplied Muslim and later European markets with Slavic, Greek and African slaves for nearly a millennium. The Fourth Crusade (1204), financed and redirected by Venice, culminated in the sack of Constantinople, the theft of the bronze horses of San Marco and systematic looting of Byzantine civilization. The Venetian name 'Venexia' has been administratively displaced by Tuscan-derived standard Italian 'Venezia' since the 19th-century unification; the Venetian language is classified as endangered.

sources.citation: Lane, F.C., Venice: A Maritime Republic, Johns Hopkins University Press, 1973
Madden, T.F., Venice: A New History, Viking, 2012

name_variants.name: Venezia
name_variants.context: Standard Italian form, became dominant after Napoleonic conquest and 1866 Italian annexation

sources.citation: Najemy, J., A History of Florence 1200-1575, Blackwell, 2006
Brucker, G., Renaissance Florence, University of California Press, 1983

ethical_notes: The Ligurian-language name 'Zena' has been progressively replaced by the standard Italian 'Genova' since the 19th-century unification and compulsory schooling; Ligurian is classified by UNESCO as a definitely endangered language. The Republic of Genoa (c. 1099-1797) was a major participant in the medieval Black Sea and Atlantic slave trade — its colony of Caffa (Crimea) was a principal slave market supplying the Egyptian Mamluk sultanate, and Genoese navigators including Columbus were central to early Atlantic slaving enterprises.

sources.citation: Epstein, S., Genoa and the Genoese 958-1528, University of North Carolina Press, 1996
Kirk, T.A., Genoa and the Sea, Johns Hopkins University Press, 2005

name_variants.name: Genova
name_variants.context: Standard Italian form, dominant since Napoleonic period
